coredata.dc:title: Construction of stable passivation sites in coatings by functionalized surface modified fly ash and its unique anti-corrosion properties

coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with non-covalently functionalized imidazole groups on their surface were chemically grafted onto fly ash filler via hydrothermal synthesis and equivalent-volume impregnation. An epoxy composite coating was then prepared by spraying. The I-CFA filler uses citric acid as carbon source and imidazole as nitrogen source, and a large number of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots are grown in-situ in the fly ash surface and inside, thereby improving the compatibility of the filler and epoxy resin. In addition, when the coating is damaged, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots will be released, and the imidazole groups on their surface can act as corrosion inhibitors to chemically adsorb Fe atoms on the surface of the substrate, and a continuous complexation reaction can be formed to form a passivation protective film grown in situ, which is adsorbed on the surface of carbon steel, thus playing a good barrier effect and slowing down the erosion of corrosive media. Compared with pure epoxy coating, the impedance modulus of I-CFA composite epoxy coating after double modification is 1000 times higher at common temperature condition, and 105–106 times higher at harsh CO2-rich high temperature and high pressure conditions. This study is expected to provide new insights into the preparation of fly ash coatings with unique functions.

head.ce:title: Construction of stable passivation sites in coatings by functionalized surface modified fly ash and its unique anti-corrosion properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with non-covalently functionalized imidazole groups on their surface were chemically grafted onto fly ash filler via hydrothermal synthesis and equivalent-volume impregnation. An epoxy composite coating was then prepared by spraying. The I-CFA filler uses citric acid as carbon source and imidazole as nitrogen source, and a large number of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots are grown in-situ in the fly ash surface and inside, thereby improving the compatibility of the filler and epoxy resin. In addition, when the coating is damaged, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots will be released, and the imidazole groups on their surface can act as corrosion inhibitors to chemically adsorb Fe atoms on the surface of the substrate, and a continuous complexation reaction can be formed to form a passivation protective film grown in situ, which is adsorbed on the surface of carbon steel, thus playing a good barrier effect and slowing down the erosion of corrosive media. Compared with pure epoxy coating, the impedance modulus of I-CFA composite epoxy coating after double modification is 1000 times higher at common temperature condition, and 105–106 times higher at harsh CO2-rich high temperature and high pressure conditions. This study is expected to provide new insights into the preparation of fly ash coatings with unique functions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal materials are widely used in national economic life because of their high strength, high toughness, high ductility and easy conduction and heat transfer. However, metal is prone to corrosion, which will not only affect its function and service life, but also cause environmental pollution, and even bring security risks and economic losses [1], so it is imperative to protect metal. Among various corrosion protection strategies, current mainstream technologies include altering the structural design and controlling metals [2], surface coating protection technology [3–5], electrochemical protection law [6,7], and adding corrosion inhibitors [8,9]. The epoxy coating in the surface coating protection technology is widely used in the field of corrosion prevention because of its durable wear resistance, good heat resistance, electrical insulation and low cost [10]. However, during the curing process of epoxy resin, micro-pores, cracks and holes will occur in the coating due to solvent volatilization, which is easy to penetrate the corrosive medium, resulting in the decline of the barrier performance of the coating [11,12]. To solve this problem, Yang et al. creatively modified graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) using chitosan (CS) to prepare a graphitic carbon nitride enhanced corrosion resistant EP/g-C₃N₄@CS nanocomposite coating. In the molecular structure of CS, there are functional groups such as hydroxyl and amino. These reactive groups give CS high reactivity and affinity, enabling it to effectively interact with the metal surface and then form a stable protective film [13]. Yu et al. used polydopamine and silane coupling agents to double modify boron nitride and prepare an epoxy composite coating, forming a polydopamine layer on the surface of BN, and enhancing the corrosion resistance of the coating by using the π-π stacking derived from the polydopamine layer and the strong interface interaction of van der Waals forces [14]. In summary, finding a kind of inorganic filler with good performance and low price has become the top priority.

In recent years, the application of fly ash in many fields has made remarkable progress [15], but the original fly ash is a loose, porous and irregularly shaped tiny particle with large fineness and particle size [16]. If the fly ash is directly added to the coating as a filler, its compatibility with the epoxy resin is poor, which makes the filler easy to agglomerate, resulting in uneven dispersion. The corrosion resistance of coating is seriously affected. The filler can be modified functionally, and functional groups can be constructed on the surface of the filler to improve the dispersion compatibility between the inorganic filler and the epoxy resin and improve the anti-corrosion performance of the coating [17]. There are a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the structure of citric acid. These functional groups can cross-link with epoxy resin to improve the dispersion of filler in coating. At the same time, citric acid can also electrolyze citrate ions in solution, which will form a blocking complex with metal cations, thereby slowing down corrosion [18]. Li et al. synthesized a polyaniline coating with citric acid as doping state (PANI-CA) by chemical oxidation. The research shows that only when the concentration of citric acid is about 4 %, the anti-corrosion performance of the coating can be effectively improved, and too little PANI-CA will limit the promotion effect. Excessive PANI-CA will lead to excessive surface roughness and micro-pores, which will affect the anti-corrosion performance [19]. Ren et al., using triethylenetetramine as nitrogen source and citric acid as carbon source, synthesized nitrogen-doped blue CDs by hydrothermal method. The surface of the CDS is rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine and other reactive groups, which can improve the comprehensive performance of fillers [20]. Therefore, citric acid has certain limitations in improving the anticorrosion performance of the coating. It is necessary to use a substance with excellent compatibility as a nitrogen source and supplement it with modification to generate carbon quantum dots to improve the anticorrosion performance of the coating.

Imidazole can improve the compatibility between different polymers, improve the strength and toughness of the material, and can be used as a nitrogen source to form carbon quantum dots with citric acid [21]. Carbon quantum dots have excellent corrosion inhibition properties and can be loaded into the composite coating to build a coating system with passivation properties [22–24], while active nitrogen atoms in imidazole can act as a bridge between carbon dots and the fly ash filler modified with citric acid. Therefore, imidazole can graft carbon dots on the fly ash modified with citric acid, graft nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots to enhance the interface bond between the filler and the epoxy resin, and form a protective film on the metal surface to protect the metal substrate from further corrosion [25,26].

In this study, the porous solid waste fly ash was used as the base filler. Citric acid was used to introduce functional groups on the surface of the filler, and imidazole was used to make carbon quantum dots on the surface of fly ash and in situ growth of holes. The effects of functional groups on the interface properties of fillers and resins were investigated. At the same time, the compatibility of filler and epoxy resin was analyzed by testing the dispersion of filler and crosslinking of filler and resin. The wear resistance and corrosion performance of different types of filler and resin coating were studied by friction test and electrochemical corrosion test under ordinary environment. The results showed that the interfacial interaction of the modified coating (CFA) with citric acid and the modified coating (I-CFA) with citric acid and imidazole improved the compatibility between the filler and epoxy resin, and the friction mass loss of the I-CFA coating was reduced to 0.0083 g, which was 52.8 % lower than that of the pure epoxy coating. The I-CFA coating still showed excellent shielding properties after soaking in 3.5 % sodium chloride solution for 90 days, and the impedance modulus at low frequency was about 5.98 × 1010 Ω·cm2. Subsequently, the anticorrosion properties of CFA coating and I-CFA coating under harsh environment were further studied. The impedance modulus of I-CFA coating at low frequency was about 2.31 × 1010 Ω·cm2, indicating that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots modified fly ash could have a positive effect on coating protection of epoxy resin under extreme environment. Meanwhile, SVET was used to explore the passivation effect of the modified coating. This work provides a new method for the functional modification of fly ash and the realization of strong anti-corrosion effect under harsh conditions.

The metal substrate used in the experiment (grade: Q235, quality grade: B, C ≤ 0.20 %, Si ≤ 0.35 %, Mn ≤ 1.4 %, S ≤ 0.045 %, P ≤ 0.045 %, 100 mm × 100 mm × 2 mm) carbon steel was purchased from Hebei Iron and Steel Group (China). Ethyl acetate was purchased from Liaoning Quanrui Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). E51 epoxy resin and 905 curing agents were purchased from Wuxi Qianguang Chemical Raw Materials Co., LTD. (China). Tween 80 was purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Citric acid was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Fly ash purchased from Yanxi Mining Co., Ltd. (China). Imidazole was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China).

The CFA filler was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis method and equivalent-volume impregnation method. The specific process was as follows: First, the crude sample was pretreated; the fly ash was ball-milled, and the treated fly ash was calcined in the Muffle furnace at 700–800 °C for 6 h (to remove the impurities in the fly ash). Secondly, 2.1 g citric acid and 2.1 g fly ash were dissolved in 20 ml distilled water by a high-precision electronic analytical balance, and the solution of fully dissolved and mixed citric acid fly ash was obtained after stirring for 45 min. The solution was then transferred to a high-pressure Teflon-lined reactor and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the autoclave is naturally cooled to room temperature. Then, the CFA powder was obtained by using 0.1 μm filter membrane and drying in 80 °C oven. Finally, according to the saturation measure of citric acid in fly ash, 2 ml distilled water and 1.5 g citric acid were fully dissolved and added to the powder in the previous step. After soaking for 6 h in the same volume, the citric acid molecules were extracted and filtered and dried with 0.1 μm filter membrane, and the citric acid molecules were filled into the intermolecular pores of the modified fly ash.

Imidazole aqueous solution is alkaline, acid-base neutralization reaction will occur when it is mixed with citric acid. Excessive imidazole should be added to remove excess acid when preparing secondary modified I-CFA filler. The specific experimental process is as follows: First, 2.1 g acid-modified fly ash filler were added into 0.125 g/ml of 20 ml imidazole solution, stirred for 45 min, and then transferred to a high pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the autoclave is naturally cooled to room temperature. Then, after filtration with 0.1 μm filter membrane, put in 80 °C oven drying. Finally, the fly ash was immersed in 2 ml imidazole solution of 0.6 g/ml for an equal volume of 6 h, and then suction filtration, filtered and dried to obtain I-CFA powder.

The preparation steps of modified fly ash organic coating are shown as follows. The coating was prepared by first mixing 0.8 g epoxy resin, 0.8 g 905 curing agent, and 1 drop of Tween80 surfactant in 10 ml ethyl acetate, followed by 15-min ultrasonication to form an emulsified resin solution. Subsequently, 0.2 g filler (fly ash, citric acid-modified fly ash, or citric acid/imidazole-modified fly ash) was added and ultrasonically dispersed. Q235B steel substrates were polished (240–2000 mesh sandpaper), cleaned via ethanol ultrasonication (99.5 %, 10 min), and spray-coated with the prepared solution under 100–120 °C heating. After leveling for 12 h, the coatings were thermally cured at 60 °C (1 h) and 180 °C (2 h). Four variants were fabricated: EP (no filler), FA-EP, CFA-EP, and I-CFA-EP, with final thicknesses of 130 ± 10 μm (general corrosion tests) or 190 ± 10 μm (high-temperature/pressure CO2 tests) (Fig. 1 ).

The chemical composition of the fillers FA, CFA and I-CFA were obtained by the Tensor27 Fourier Transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) in the range of 4000–500 cm−1, with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1 and a scanning rate of 0.2 cm/s, and tested using KBr particles. The surface morphology of the filler powder was observed by ZEISS scanning electron microscope (SEM).

In order to verify the dispersibility of functional fillers in aqueous solvents, dispersion tests were conducted on the fillers. 1 g of FA, CFA and I-CFA were placed in three 20 ml reagent bottles, and 1 g E51 epoxy resin and 10 ml anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %) were added to them for ultrasonic dispersion. Then, the mixed solution dispersed by ultrasound for 10 min was transferred to the sample bottle, and after thorough shaking, it was left to stand for settlement experiment, and recorded every 40 min.

In order to study whether the I-CFA powder filler can release and act as a barrier to corrosive media when permeated by corrosive media, the release test was carried out. 1 g of I-CFA fillers were placed in three 20 ml sample bottles pre-filled with distilled water, ethyl acetate and anhydrous ethanol, and were vigorously shaken to make the fillers and solvents fully mixed and then stood for observation. The changes were recorded every 40 min. After 80 min, the three samples were extracted and filtered with 0.45 μm filter membrane, and the solvent was drained and transferred to the surface dish and dried in the oven at 80 °C for >12 h. The thoroughly dried fillers powder was obtained and weighed, and the mass loss of the three samples was recorded.

To verify the crosslinking performance of functional fillers with resin and curing agent, the crosslinking test of fillers was carried out. 1 g of FA, CFA and I-CFA were placed in three 50 ml beakers with 20 ml ethyl acetate and 1 g E51 epoxy resin, stirred for 45 min, then transferred to a high pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoron, reacted at 180 °C for 3 h, and then transferred to a 20 ml sample bottle after the reactor cooled, and a control group was set up. The cross-linking test was performed after 10 min of ultrasonic dispersion. Repeat the above steps for crosslinking test of filler and curing agent.

The wear resistance of the coating was tested by Taber wear testing machine. The friction test speed was 3000 r/h, the grinding wheel type was H22, and the weight was 1 kg. Before the test, the coating is pre-rubbed to reduce the experimental error, and then the surface debris is wiped off, the mass is weighed, and the formal test is conducted. Each sample is subjected to three friction tests, and the mass is weighed by subtraction every 25 min, and the average of the three measurement results is taken. The morphology of wear trajectories on the surface of different coating substrates and the morphology characteristics of fracture cross sections of coating substrates were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The mass loss of wear surfaces under different friction cycles was recorded. The morphology characteristics of functional fly ash fillers and epoxy resin substrates were analyzed.

To study the anticorrosive properties of EP, FA-EP, CFA-EP and I-CFA-EP coatings, the anticorrosive properties of the coatings on the substrate under normal and harsh conditions were studied by EIS test results. With platinum wire as the counter electrode and saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, the measurement frequency range was 105–10−2 Hz, and the sinusoidal signal perturbation was 10 mV. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on the coating soaked in 3.5 % NaCl solution at room temperature using an electrochemical workstation (PARSTAT 3000 A). The EIS test area of the coating was 12.57 cm2, and the EIS test results were fitted by ZSimpWin. The scanning Vibration electrode technology (SVET, VERSASCAN) was used to test the corrosion on the surface of different coated substrates. During the test, the average distance from the electrode tip to the coating surface was always 200 μm. The scanning probe moved at 50 μm/s, and the data was detected at 21 × 21 scanning points in an area of 1 mm2. High temperature and high pressure carbon dioxide corrosion experiment was carried out using a high-pressure reactor, and 3.5 % NaCl solution was added to ensure that the solution soaked the sample, and then the high-pressure reactor was sealed at 80 °C and 2 MPa for 32 h. After the test, the anti-corrosion properties of different coatings were tested by EIS results of the coatings. After the EIS test, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to observe the surface, interface with substrate and cross section morphology of the stripped coating samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface measurement and morphology characterization of fillers
ce:section.ce:para: The micro morphologies of FA, CFA and I-CFA fillers were measured by scanning electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 , fly ash particles exhibit typical spherical or irregular shapes. After enlarging the region, a large number of pores are found, and serious agglomeration occurs. CFA fillers show irregular shape with tiny pores, slightly agglomerating phenomenon, and the surface tends to be smooth. There are some uneven bumps on the surface, which are carbon quantum dots grown in situ, which proves that the surface modification of fly ash by citric acid is successful. The I-CFA filler presents a broken polyhedron shape and a relatively smooth surface morphology, showing a unique coating shape. After the local area of the I-CFA filler is enlarged, there is a flocculation structure on the surface of the fly ash, indicating that the fly ash exhibits a strong tendency to combine with citric acid and imidazole, and there are some uneven overlapping protruded on the surface. This is the point of nitrogen doped carbon grown in situ, demonstrating the success of further modification. As shown in Fig. S1, the infrared spectrum test of FA, CFA and I-CFA fillers was carried out, and the results further verified that the modification of CFA and I-CFA fillers was successful.

The dispersion of the filler can be judged by comparing the clarification of the solution. As shown in Fig. 3(a1), as shown in Fig. 3(a1), each sample bottle just taken out is in a turbid state after ultrasonic dispersion. After 40 min, as shown in Fig. 3(a2), the upper layer of unmodified fly ash and acid-modified fillers appeared clear, and the lower layer of fillers settled to the bottom of the bottle, while the solution system of I-CFA fillers still showed turbidity. After 80 min, as shown in Fig. 3(a3), both the unmodified fly ash and the acid-modified fillers showed an obvious settling trend, the lower layer sediments tended to be complete, and the overall solution was clear and transparent, while the I-CFA fillers still did not have obvious supernatant, which indicated that the I-CFA fillers were compared with the FA and CFA before modification. Excellent dispersion in ethanol solution. This is due to the addition of hydrophilic groups such as amino group and carboxyl group to the functional modification, which improves its dispersibility in epoxy resin. The results above show that FA fillers have better water compatibility after functional modification, which is conducive to the uniform dispersion of fillers in the coating.

Whether I-CFA can be released in the case of corrosive media penetration and play a blocking and slowing down the effect of corrosion prevention can be analyzed and judged by comparing the packing settlement and mass loss of the three samples. As shown in Fig. 3(b1), each sample bottle just standing was only in a turbid state after vigorous oscillation and was precipitated for 40 min under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3(b2), I-CFA fillers did not precipitate significantly in distilled water and anhydrous ethanol, on the contrary, they precipitated significantly in ethyl acetate solvent. After continued standing for 40 min, as shown in Fig. 3(b3), the I-CFA fillers showed only slight color changes in distilled water and anhydrous ethanol, while the solution became clearly clarified in ethyl acetate solvent. The results indicated that the Speed of clarification of I-CFA fillers in solvent ethyl acetate was much higher than that in anhydrous ethanol and water (corrosive environment). Secondly, as shown in Fig. 3(b3), the mass loss of the I-CFA filler after filtration and drying is only 0.001 g in ethyl acetate, while the mass loss in distilled water and anhydrous ethanol is 0.103 g and 0.102 g respectively, which proves that the mass release of the I-CFA filler in ethyl acetate does not take place. In distilled water and anhydrous ethanol, 10 % of the filler is released. The above test results show that the I-CFA filler can release nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots only in corrosive environment, and the dispersion effect in ethyl acetate solvent is due to its dispersion effect rather than release property. According to the literature, when the corrosive medium penetrates the coating, the filler will release and play an anti-corrosion effect [27], indicating that the I-CFA filler has the same performance.

After the reaction, the three E51 epoxy resin samples were placed in three 20 ml sample bottles, and the non-reaction group N-FA (FA filler did not react with E51 epoxy resin in the high-pressure reactor, and the substance retained its original physical properties) was set up for control experiment. Then, the sample was transferred to the sample bottle after ultrasound, and after vigorous vibration, the sedimentation experiment was carried out at 25 °C, and the record was made every 40 min. Under the same conditions, the cross-linking between the filler and the resin was judged by comparing the clarification of the solution. As shown in Fig. 4(a1), each sample bottle just take out was in a dark brown and cloudy state after oscillation. After 40 min, FA did not change significantly, the color of CFA changed from dark brown to light brown, and the color of I-CFA changed from dark brown to light yellow. This is because citric acid grafts hydrophilic groups on the surface of CFA and I-CFA, which greatly improves the crosslinking of CFA and I-CFA fillers with epoxy resin. After 80 min, the FA still did not change significantly, the color of CFA changed from light brown to dark yellow, and at the same time, part of the clarity was restored, but it was still very different from the I-CFA and the control group N-FA. The I-CFA changed from light yellow to transparent, and the solution became clarified, which was not much different from the control group N-FA. These phenomena indicate that the carboxyl group on the surface grafted citric acid enhances the crosslinking between the CFA and I-CFA fillers and the epoxy resin. In particular, the imidazoline-modified I-CFA generates a lot of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots in situ and introduces a large number of amino groups, which further enhances the crosslinking between the I-CFA fillers and the epoxy resin. In addition, the 1 g E51 epoxy resin was replaced with 1 g 905 curing agent and the above operation was repeated. As shown in Fig. 4(b1), there was no significant difference between the 905 curing agent and the three fillers in the initial state and the state after 40 min and 80 min. The color of the suspension was also similar to that of the pure curing agent. The results showed that the three samples formed a block and settled at the bottom of the high pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene at 180 °C, and the fillers and curing agent were cross-linked. However, the samples without reaction remained cloudy in 0–80 min, indicating that the curing agent and the filler were simply adsorbed and mixed, and there was no cross-linking reaction.

Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 describe the average mass loss of different coating samples and the microstructure of the composite coating after Taber wear test and analyze the direct contribution of different fillers to improving the wear resistance of the coating. Meanwhile, adhesion test results of coating for various coated substrate samples are presented. The experimental standard employed was SYI·6717-2016. Among these samples, the I-CFA-EP coating exhibited the highest tensile maximum force of 4417.455 N, followed by the CFA-EP coating with a tensile maximum force of 2695.946 N. These findings demonstrate the superior adhesion between the I-CFA-EP and CFA-EP coatings and their respective substrates. Such excellent adhesion is also a critical factor contributing to the coatings' outstanding corrosion resistance. In contrast, the EP and FA-EP coatings displayed relatively weaker adhesion performance, particularly the EP coating, which achieved a tensile maximum force of only 2695.946 N. These results suggest that the overall mechanical properties of the FA and EP coatings are less favorable compared to those of the I-CFA-EP and CFA-EP coatings. The mass loss of EP coating is 0.0176 g, and the wear marks of EP coating are very messy and have serious gully marks, indicating that the wear degree of pure epoxy coating is very serious. In addition, a large number of wear products were accumulated in the grooves of the coating, and a broken morphological structure appeared near the gullies. At the same time, the volume of wear products was large and the coating was ground out of many small holes, indicating that the internal structure of the pure epoxy coating was unstable.

When the unmodified fly ash filler is added to the coating as a filler, the mass loss of the coating is reduced to 0.0152 g, which is only 13.6 % less than that of the EP coating. The wear resistance of the coating is improved, which is due to the hard texture of the fly ash material itself. However, under the electron microscope, the agglomeration phenomenon still showed obvious strip-shaped gullies, which indicated that the lubrication characteristics of unmodified fly ash materials were poor. Meanwhile, the wear area of the coating showed intricate wear marks and large pieces of wear products. Combined with the experimental characterization of dispersion and crosslinking, it was found that the fly ash was not uniformly dispersed in the coating and had poor compatibility with the resin, indicating that the addition of unmodified fly ash material could not make up for the defects of the unstable structure of the pure epoxy coating.

To improve the uniform dispersion degree of the fly ash filler in the coating, the fly ash was hydrophilicity modified with citric acid, and oxygen-containing groups such as carboxyl group and hydroxyl group were grafted on the surface of the filler to make it better dispersed into the epoxy resin system, and the compatibility between the fly ash filler and the epoxy resin was improved. After adding CFA filler composite material to the coating, the mass loss of the coating is reduced to 0.0120 g, which is reduced by 31.8 % compared with EP coating. Simultaneously, the microscopic wear marks are smooth and there are no obvious traces of grooving, indicating that the CFA filler composite filler has good lubricity, which solves the problems of the above coating with chaotic scratches and obvious grooving. This shows that the introduction of hydrophilic groups in citric acid greatly improves the lubricity of the filler. However, some fragments of wear products were accumulated in the grooves of the coating, indicating that the internal structure of the CFA-EP coating was not unstable, so the crosslinking of fly ash and epoxy resin after acid modification was limited, resulting in insufficient density inside the coating. There are still many large wear products and black punctate holes on the surface, indicating that there are still some microporous defects on the CFA filler composite coating. The combination of dispersibility and crosslinking experiments showed that acid modification improved the dispersibility of fly ash composite fillers and the crosslinking with epoxy resin, but it was difficult to build a better fillers dense layer inside the coating when only citric acid modified fly ash fillers were added.

In order to build a stable and compact fillers network inside the coating, CFA fillers was doped with carbon quantum dots by imidazole, and a large number of carbon quantum dots were grown in situ by combining the surface of the fillers and the carboxyl group and the amino group in the imidazole, so that they could build a better fillers dense layer inside the coating. After the I-CFA filler composite was added to the coating, the mass loss of the coating was reduced to 0.0083 g, which was reduced by 52.8 % compared with the EP coating. In addition, the microscopic wear marks are very smooth with no obvious wear marks or linear grooves, and the surface is smoother with fewer bumps. This shows that the introduction of amino group in imidazole makes the I-CFA filler compound have better lubricity than other coatings. Meanwhile, there are no obvious holes and the wear products are very small. According to the experimental characterization of dispersibility and crosslinking, the dispersibility of I-CFA filler composite and the crosslinking of resin are the best among the four coatings. This shows that the introduction of imidazole makes a large number of carbon quantum dots grow in-situ on the surface and inside of the acid-modified fly ash, which not only improves the degree of dispersion of the filler, but also enhances the degree of wear resistance of the coating, makes the internal structure of the coating more stable, and establishes a stable and dense filler network with excellent performance.

The dispersion and crosslinking of fillers in resin matrix and the barrier effect of fillers seriously affect the anticorrosive properties of coatings. Therefore, we observed the surface of pure epoxy, fly ash, acid-modified composite and fully modified composite coatings by scanning electron microscopy, and analyzed the properties of these two aspects of the coatings. As can be seen from Fig. 6(a1–a3), the surface of the pure epoxy coating is smooth, but there are many holes (shown in the circles), which may be due to the poor crosslinking of the resin and the curing agent, and this is also a defect of the E51 epoxy resin material. After adding fly ash powder into the epoxy matrix, due to the poor compatibility between fly ash and epoxy resin matrix, fly ash is seriously agglomerated, there are obvious gaps between fly ash and epoxy resin, and the interface interaction is poor, and a large number of holes are caused (as shown in the dotted circle). After the citric acid was splice on the surface of the coating and impregnated inside the coating by hydrothermal synthesis, chemical grafting and chemical modification, the agglomeration of fly ash was improved a lot due to the good compatibility of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of the citric acid with the water-based resin matrix, but the surface was still rough, the interface interaction was general, and there were still some holes (as shown in the dotted circle in c2). Finally, imidazole and citric acid were reacted into carbon quantum dots by equivalent-volume impregnation method, and many carbon quantum dots were in-situ grown inside the coating. The compatibility between fly ash and epoxy resin matrix was greatly improved due to the generation of many hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl group, carbonyl group and hydroxyl group, and there was no obvious agglomeration in the system, the surface had small debris and the interface interaction was good and no obvious holes. Therefore, the two modifications of imidazole and citric acid allow a large number of carbon quantum dots to grow in-situ inside and on the surface of fly ash, and introduce oxygen-containing groups and amine groups, which can cross-link with the curing agent and epoxy resin at the same time to produce covalent bonds, enhance the cross-linking density, and improve the rough coating of the original fly ash, poor interface interaction and holes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of corrosion resistance of coatings in ordinary environment
ce:section.ce:para: To test the long-term anticorrosion ability of the coating, the prepared coating sample was immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using a three-electrode system. The obtained electrochemical data were fitted by Zview software, and corresponding equivalent circuit models were matched respectively, as shown in Fig. S2. In the figure, the dots represent the original data, and the lines represent the fitting data processed by ZSimpWin software. All samples are tested two times to ensure the reliability of the data, so as to judge the corrosion resistance of the coating.

In the Nyquist diagram, the diameter of the capacitor arc is closely related to the corrosion process [28], while the low-frequency impedance modulus |Z|0.01 Hz in the Bode diagram is an important parameter to characterize the protection performance of the coating [29,30]. As shown in Fig. 7 , after initial soaking, all samples have large capacitance arc radius and high low-frequency impedance modulus, and the value of |Z|0.01 Hz of the coating is close to 1011 Ω·cm2, showing clear capacitance behavior and barrier protection [31]. After soaking for 45 days, the radius of capacitor arc of most coatings decreased gradually with the extension of soaking time, and |Z|0.01 Hz decreased. EP coating fell to 1.54 × 109 Ω·cm2, a decrease of two orders of magnitude; The CFA-EP coating fell to 2.04 × 1010 Ω·cm2, down by an order of magnitude. This indicates that the corrosive medium has penetrated into the interface between the coating and the substrate. The breakpoint frequency is the frequency corresponding to the −45° phase Angle in the baud Angle diagram, which reflects the micro-stratification degree of the coating [31–33]. EP and CFA-EP coatings have a breakpoint frequency of 0.794 Hz and 0.025 Hz respectively, which indicates that the internal layer area of the coating is large. After soaking for 90 days, the arc radius of most coatings decreases further with the extension of soaking time, and |Z|0.01 Hz further decrease. In particular, the |Z|0.01 Hz value of EP coating drops to 3.5 × 108 Ω·cm2, which is three orders of magnitude lower than the initial value. The radius of capacitor arc is very small, and the breakpoint frequency is further increased, indicating that serious corrosion reaction has occurred at the interface between the coating and the substrate. Pitting corrosion is characterized by the formation of local erosion pits on the metal surface. As shown in Fig. S4, EP coating has a serious pitting phenomenon. The small black spots in the figure can partially isolate the metal substrate from the corrosive medium [34], but such corrosion is difficult to detect and poses a great threat to the structural integrity [33]. The capacitance arc radius and impedance modulus of FA-EP coating gradually decrease. Combined with the cross-linking experiment and SEM scanning electron microscope test, it is found that the internal structure of FA-EP coating is not dense, and it is difficult to form a dense shielding network. Different from the above two coatings, the impedance value of CFA-EP coating rises, the radius of capacitor arc rebounds, and the breakpoint frequency “disappears”. Combined with SVET experimental characterization, this is due to the corrosion products generated by the reaction of CFA-EP coating and corrosive substances play a blocking role, extending the corrosion path, and therefore increasing the corrosion resistance. For the I-CFA-EP coating, the arc radius of the capacitor decreases slightly with time, and the arc radius of the capacitor rebounds at 90 days, and the impedance modulus increases. Combined with the characterization of SVET experiment, although corrosion reaction occurs at the interface between the coating and the substrate, the carbon quantum dots on the surface of the coating react with corrosive substances, thus forming a dense passivated film. Delay the corrosion of corrosive substances on the metal substrate. It is worth noting that although both CFA-EP and I-CFA-EP coatings have the effect of enhancing the corrosion resistance of coatings, the capacitance arc radius of CFA-EP coatings during the test is generally weaker than that of I-CFA-EP coatings, indicating that fly ash fillers modified only with citric acid are difficult to form a stable and dense filler shielding layer inside the coating. On this basis, imidazole doping carbon quantum dots can form a uniform passivation film on the surface of the coating and build a solid and dense filler shielding layer inside the coating, thereby improving the corrosion resistance of the epoxy composite coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion resistance testing of coatings in harsh environments
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the corrosion resistance of the coatings in harsh environments, four coatings were immersed in a CO2-rich 3.5% NaCl solution and tested at 80 °C and 2 MPa for 32 h. As can be seen from Fig. 8 , the low-frequency impedance modulus of the I-CFA-EP coating is higher, the value of |Z|0.01 Hz is 2.31 × 1010, and the radius of the capacitor arc is larger. In contrast, the arc radius of the other three coatings is significantly smaller and the low-frequency impedance modulus is reduced. Specifically, the |Z|0.01 Hz value of EP coating decreases by two orders of magnitude, the |Z|0.01 Hz value of FA-EP coating decreases by four orders of magnitude, and the |Z|0.01 Hz value of CFA-EP coating falls to 104 Ω·cm2. When the value of |Z|0.01 Hz is <105, it is considered that the protective effect has been lost [35]. I-CFA-EP coating as a coating with excellent anti-corrosion performance, its phase angle value is close to 90° in a wide frequency range. For the coatings of the other three coatings, their phase Angle in the high frequency region is significantly reduced, and the barrier performance of the surface coating is decreased.

In order to more directly reflect the degree of corrosion of the coating, scanning electron microscope was used to observe the microscopic morphology of the coating after the corrosion resistance experiment in harsh environment. Figs. 9, 10 and 11
show the SEM images of the coating surface, the interface between the coating and the substrate, and the cross section of the coating after testing each coating in a composite environment of 2 MPa pure CO2 and 3.5wt.% NaCl solution. There are many corrosion products on the surface of EP coating, accompanied by serious pitting phenomenon, and even holes appear in some areas, at the interface with the substrate, an obvious ball of corrosion product is formed, and the cross-sectional coating has a large area of convex. The above phenomenon shows that the corrosion resistance of pure epoxy coating is weak under harsh conditions. The surface of the FA-EP coating is relatively uniform and there are fewer corrosion products. The corrosion products on the binding surface of the substrate are abrasive and holes appear on the side, the content of Fe and Cl in the sample showed an increasing trend compared with the pure epoxy coating, indicating that the corrosion was more serious than that of the pure epoxy coating, indicating that the coating has poor densification, has formed corrosion channels, and has weak protection performance on the metal substrate. On the front of the CFA-EP coating, there are small holes, there are corrosion products around them, and the samples contain the highest contents of Fe and CI elements compared with the other three coatings, indicating that the areas around these holes are seriously corroded, and the corrosion products on the joint surface of the CFA-EP coating show scratches, and there are small holes and slight pitting corrosion around the surface, and the overlap degree of the side coating is low. Combined with the EIS results, the CFA-EP coating has lost its anti-corrosion performance under harsh environment. On the other hand, the front of the I-CFA-EP coating has a slight pitting phenomenon, and the corrosion products on the bonding surface of the substrate are less and there are no obvious holes, secondly, the content of Fe and Cl elements in the corrosion products of the coatings is the lowest in the four coatings, indicating that the corrosion degree of these I-CFA-EP coatings is lighter. Combined with SVET characterization, a dense passivation film is formed at the interface between the coating and the substrate during the corrosion process, which plays a role of barrier and corrosion inhibition, and the corrosive medium channel is difficult to form, which makes it have excellent anti-corrosion performance in harsh environments.

After the EIS test at room temperature, the coating on the surface of the metal substrate was stripped by liquid nitrogen cooling method, and then different steel substrate samples were immersed in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution and SVET was used to scan the micro-corrosion signals of different substrate samples. By comparing the potential signal strength of the samples, it can reflect the corrosion degree of the steel plate and the corrosion activity of the anode and cathode [36]. The test results are shown in Fig. 12 The EP sample has a large area of strong anode reaction signal, but no obvious cathodic electrical signal region, indicating that at this time, some Fe atoms in the steel plate have lost electrons and become free Fe ions, that is, anodic dissolution occurs [37], combined with the SEM images (a1–a3 in Fig. 9), serious pitting phenomena on the surface of the steel substrate can be observed, which indicates that the EP coating has lost its shielding effect before SVET test. The metal substrate of FA-EP sample also showed a strong corrosion signal, indicating that FA filler did not play a good anti-corrosion inhibition effect on the metal substrate. In contrast to the above two coatings, the steel substrate of the CFA-EP and I-CFA-EP samples showed a strong cathodic electrical signal region, showing a low corrosion activity, indicating that no Fe ion loss occurred here, and the coating produced a passivation layer effect on the surface of the steel substrate [38].The cathode and anode corrosion signals on the substrate surface of the two are evenly distributed and the anode corrosion depth is shallow, which is due to incomplete passivation. A small amount of corrosive media eventually reaches the surface of the substrate not protected by the passivation film, which increases the corrosion potential of the metal substrate. However, due to the existence of the passivation film, the corrosive media cannot damage the substrate, and a large area of cathode passivation is still presented [39]. Furthermore, compared with the I-CFA-EP characterization results, the SVET diagram of CFA-EP steel substrate has more anodic corrosion signal regions, and the microscopic image of the interface between the coating and the substrate (c1–c3 in Fig. 10) shows that there are a large number of corrosion products accumulated along the grinding marks, the cathode and anode corrosion signals of the I-CFA-EP coating is more evenly distributed. Cathode electrical signals are more dense. These results show that the citric acid modified filler can passivate the steel substrate and slow down the corrosion process. Secondly, Fe3+ possesses 3d empty orbitals and can act as an electron acceptor, while N-CQDs have abundant and diverse functional groups (such as hydroxyl (–OH), amino (−NH2) and carboxyl (–COOH)) which can serve as electron donors. Therefore, they can easily form stable complexes [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 14(c), nitro-doped carbon quantum dots grown in situ were further modified by adding imidazole, and their own imidazole groups could be used as corrosion inhibitors to chemically adsorb Fe atoms on the substrate surface [42,43], and in situ growth to form a self-assembly protective film, so that the formed protective film has a better barrier effect and corrosion inhibition effect, the anti-corrosion effect of the steel substrate is more prominent.

Fig. 13 shows the protection mechanism of epoxy composite coatings made with different types of fly ash fillers. Fig. 14 elucidates the cross-linking mechanism, anti-corrosion mechanism, and passivation effect of the resin in conjunction with the curing agent. As shown in Fig. 13(a), in an unfilled epoxy coating, corrosive substances pass through the coating unhindered to corrode the substrate. Fig. 13(b) reveals that in the epoxy resin coating with unmodified fly ash, the compatibility between the resin and the filler is weak. This low compatibility leads to uneven distribution of the filler in the coating, and corrosion channels are easy to form in some places. At the same time, the coating surface becomes loose and porous, making it easier for corrosive media to penetrate. As a result, the barrier properties of the coating are significantly affected. As shown in Fig. 13(c), in the epoxy resin coating with citric acid-modified fly ash, the compatibility of filler and resin is improved to some extent, and the inorganic filler is uniformly dispersed in the coating, thus extending the path of the corrosive medium. As shown in Fig. 14(b), the modified filler will react with free iron ions to generate corrosion products, which will further block the effect. As shown in Fig. 13(d), in the epoxy resin coating with fly ash modified by citric acid and imidazole, the dispersion of the filler in the epoxy resin has been well improved, and its dispersion in the coating is relatively uniform, which improves the compactness of the coating. As shown in Fig. 14(c), when the coating is damaged after the invasion of corrosive substances, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots in the coating will be released, and its own imidazole group acts as a corrosion inhibitor to chemically adsorb Fe atoms on the surface of the substrate, and a complex reaction occurs to form a compact passivation passivated film. In addition, with the release of carbon quantum dots, the coordination bonds of the complex keep increasing, forming a more extensive cross-linked network shielding structure. This enhances the cross-linking density of the coating near the substrate, improving the barrier performance against corrosive media such as Cl−, H2O, and CO2 (Figs. 13(d)and 14(c)), thereby reducing the electrochemical reactivity of the coating [44]. The protective film formed in this way has a cross-networking shielding structure and good corrosion inhibition.

In this study, the porous solid waste fly ash is combined with carbon quantum dots. With the help of the porous structure of the fly ash filler itself, the carbon quantum dots are grown on the inner and outer surfaces of the porous fly ash by constant volume impregnation, and carbon quantum dots are injected into the filler holes to make them grow in situ in the porous structure of the fly ash, so as to enhance the wear resistance and corrosion inhibition of the composite coating. Citric acid is the carbon source of carbon quantum dots, and there are a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on its surface. While increasing the dispersity of the filler, citrate ion can also form a closed complex with Fe ion. At the same time, imidazole, as the nitrogen source of carbon quantum dots, contains two N atoms with lone pair electrons, which is easy to form coordination bonds with Fe atoms and produce adsorption on the metal surface, changing the interface properties and charge state of the metal surface. The combined action of the two and the action of carbon quantum dots contribute to the corrosion resistance of the coating. In addition, carbon quantum dots also have strong electronegativity, which further enhances the interface bonding ability of filler and resin matrix. The hydrophilic surface groups such as carboxyl group and amino group on the surface of carbon quantum dots enhance the strong interaction between carbon quantum dots and the filler, so that the composite filler exhibits excellent corrosion inhibition activity. When the coating is damaged, the carbon quantum dots will be released, and the adjacent carbon dots released can be continuously complexed with Fe atoms on the surface of the imidazole group chemisorbed substrate, adsorbed on the surface of carbon steel, and formed a passivation film, thereby slowing down the erosion of the corrosive medium, resulting in a certain “self-healing” effect. In short, the study provides a theoretical basis for the practical application of porous solid waste fly ash filler.
